content.text: Prompt tuning (Lester et al., 2021) distils the prefix tuning idea to its minimal form: instead of prepending trainable KV vectors to every transformer layer, you prepend a small sequence of trainable embedding vectors — the "soft prompt" — only at the input layer. The model processes the soft prompt tokens alongside the real input tokens, with gradients flowing back into the soft prompt during training while every transformer weight stays frozen.

The result is an extraordinarily compact fine-tuning method: for a 10-token soft prompt on T5-XXL (11B parameters, d=4096), the trainable parameter count is just 10 × 4096 = 40,960 — 0.00037% of total parameters. The paper demonstrated that at sufficiently large model scale, this parameter budget is enough to match full fine-tuning on the SuperGLUE benchmark.
The structural difference between prompt tuning and prefix tuning is where the trainable parameters live:
: separate trainable KV pairs prepended at every attention layer. The prefix influences every layer independently.
: a single set of trainable embedding vectors prepended at layer 0. These flow through the frozen transformer like normal token embeddings; the soft prompt is only "inserted" at the very beginning.

attrs.alt: Left: Prefix tuning inserts trainable P1..P4 KV prefix vectors at every attention layer. Right: Prompt tuning inserts trainable soft tokens S1..Sl only at the input embedding layer; all attention layers are frozen and unchanged. Prompt tuning params = l×d (~10K); prefix tuning params = L×l×d×2 (~491K), making prompt tuning 50x smaller.

content.text: The parameter formula for prompt tuning is simply:
is the number of soft prompt tokens and
is the embedding dimension. Prefix tuning stores
values. For T5-XXL with 24 layers and 10 prompt tokens, this is a 48× difference in parameter count.
The Scaling Law: When It Works
The original paper's key finding is a strong scaling effect: prompt tuning performance relative to full fine-tuning depends heavily on model size.
Use prefix tuning or adapters instead
Viable if parameter budget is critical

The scaling effect arises because larger models have richer representations: even a small perturbation at the input embedding layer propagates through many parameters, providing sufficient "steering" signal. Smaller models have less redundancy and need deeper intervention (per-layer prefixes or adapter modules) to achieve the same task shift.

from peft import PromptTuningConfig, PromptTuningInit, TaskType, get_peft_model
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer, Trainer, TrainingArguments
from datasets import load_dataset

model_name = "google/flan-t5-xl"   # 3B params
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(model_name)

config = PromptTuningConfig(
    task_type=TaskType.CAUSAL_LM,
    num_virtual_tokens=20,                    # soft prompt length l
    prompt_tuning_init=PromptTuningInit.TEXT, # initialise from real text
    prompt_tuning_init_text="Classify the sentiment of this review:",
    tokenizer_name_or_path=model_name,
)

model = get_peft_model(model, config)
model.print_trainable_parameters()
# trainable params: 81,920 || all params: 2,849,443,840 || trainable%: 0.0029

dataset = load_dataset("glue", "sst2")

def preprocess(batch):
    # Soft prompt is prepended by PEFT automatically; just tokenize the input
    return tokenizer(
        batch["sentence"],
        truncation=True,
        max_length=128,
        padding="max_length",
    )

tokenized = dataset.map(preprocess, batched=True)
tokenized = tokenized.rename_column("label", "labels")
tokenized.set_format("torch", columns=["input_ids", "attention_mask", "labels"])

args = TrainingArguments(
    output_dir="./prompt-tuning-sst2",
    num_train_epochs=10,           # prompt tuning needs more epochs than full fine-tune
    per_device_train_batch_size=32,
    learning_rate=3e-2,            # much higher LR than full fine-tuning — only prompt tokens move
    warmup_ratio=0.06,
    save_strategy="epoch",
    load_best_model_at_end=True,
    metric_for_best_model="accuracy",
)

trainer = Trainer(
    model=model,
    args=args,
    train_dataset=tokenized["train"],
    eval_dataset=tokenized["validation"],
)

trainer.train()
model.save_pretrained("./prompt-tuning-sst2")

from peft import PeftModel
import torch

base = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("google/flan-t5-xl")
model = PeftModel.from_pretrained(base, "./prompt-tuning-sst2")
model.eval()

# PEFT prepends the learned soft prompt automatically during forward pass
text = "This movie was absolutely fantastic!"
inputs = tokenizer(text, return_tensors="pt")

with torch.no_grad():
    outputs = model.generate(**inputs, max_new_tokens=5)
    print(tokenizer.decode(outputs[0], skip_special_tokens=True))  # → "positive"
PEFT supports two initialisation modes for the soft prompt:

(recommended): provide a short natural-language string. The tokenizer splits it into tokens and uses their pretrained embeddings as starting points. This is semantically meaningful and converges faster, especially on smaller models.

(sampled from vocabulary): pick l tokens at random from the top-5000 vocabulary tokens and use their embeddings. Works well at large model scale where the training signal is strong enough to move random initialisations.

The Lester et al. ablation found that for T5-Small and T5-Base, text initialisation improves SuperGLUE accuracy by 3–7 points over random. For T5-XXL the difference shrinks to under 1 point — at large scale the model has enough signal to learn from any starting point.

# Text initialisation (recommended for models < 10B)
config_text = PromptTuningConfig(
    task_type=TaskType.SEQ_2_SEQ_LM,
    num_virtual_tokens=10,
    prompt_tuning_init=PromptTuningInit.TEXT,
    prompt_tuning_init_text="Summarize the following document:",
    tokenizer_name_or_path="t5-xl",
)

# Random initialisation (fine for very large models)
config_random = PromptTuningConfig(
    task_type=TaskType.SEQ_2_SEQ_LM,
    num_virtual_tokens=10,
    prompt_tuning_init=PromptTuningInit.RANDOM,
)
Two follow-up methods bridge the gap between prompt tuning and prefix tuning:

adds a lightweight LSTM or MLP reparameterisation around the prompt embeddings, similar to prefix tuning's MLP reparameterisation. The reparameteriser models dependencies between prompt tokens, allowing longer prompts to be trained more stably. Unlike prefix tuning, P-Tuning still inserts tokens only at the input (not every layer). It improves over raw prompt tuning on NLU tasks at moderate model sizes.

is essentially prefix tuning applied to NLU tasks (not generation). It prepends trainable tokens to every layer's attention, not just the input. The authors showed this closes the gap with fine-tuning on NLU benchmarks at the 300M–10B scale range where simple prompt tuning falls short — matching full fine-tuning at all tested scales.
Because soft prompts are tiny parameter files (a few KB), you can train multiple prompts for the same task and ensemble them at inference for higher accuracy:

import torch
from peft import PeftModel
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM

base = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("google/flan-t5-xl")

# Train 5 prompts with different random seeds and combine
def ensemble_predict(text, prompt_paths, k=5):
    all_logits = []
    for path in prompt_paths:
        model = PeftModel.from_pretrained(base, path)
        model.eval()
        inputs = tokenizer(text, return_tensors="pt")
        with torch.no_grad():
            out = model(**inputs)
            all_logits.append(out.logits)
    # Average logits across ensemble
    avg_logits = torch.stack(all_logits).mean(0)
    return tokenizer.decode(avg_logits.argmax(-1)[0])

# Each prompt file is ~82KB (20 tokens × 4096 dims × fp16)
# Ensemble of 5 = ~410KB total, well below even a single adapter layer

The Lester et al. paper showed that ensembling 5 prompt-tuned models of the same size produced better results than a single model fine-tuned on 5× more data — a surprising finding that highlights the benefits of prompt diversity.
Much higher than full FT — only prompt embeddings update
More epochs needed — fewer parameters to update
Large batches stabilise gradient estimate for tiny param count
Text init gives faster convergence at smaller scale
Prompt tuning's defining advantage in production is storage size. Compare per-task storage for a T5-XL (3B parameter) model with 10 tasks:
For a large enterprise model serving 1000 tenants with personalised prompts, this is the difference between terabytes and gigabytes of per-tenant storage.
: below ~7B parameters, prompt tuning lags full fine-tuning by several accuracy points on most benchmarks. Use prefix tuning or adapters for models under 3B.
: soft prompt tokens occupy context window slots. For 20 prompt tokens with a 2K context limit, you lose 1% of the window. For long-document tasks this is noticeable.

: because the soft prompt only enters at layer 0, deep layers of the model receive no direct conditioning signal — they only see the propagated effect of the soft prompt. Complex tasks may need per-layer influence (prefix tuning).
: with so few parameters, the optimiser makes very small progress per step. Prompt tuning needs more epochs than full fine-tuning or LoRA to converge — typically 10–30× more.

An interesting question is: what do the soft prompt tokens actually learn to encode? Lester et al. investigated by finding the nearest real vocabulary tokens to each trained soft prompt embedding. The nearest neighbors are often thematically related to the task — a summarisation prompt's tokens cluster near words like "brief", "summary", "extract", and "key" — suggesting the soft prompt learns to encode task semantics in embedding space.

However, the learned embeddings are not constrained to the token vocabulary. They can occupy arbitrary positions in embedding space — including regions that no real token maps to. This is both a strength (the prompt can encode meaning unreachable by any real token sequence) and a weakness (the learned prompt is not human-readable or interpretable).

Because soft prompts are trained in embedding space, you can try transferring a prompt trained on one task as an initialisation for a related task. This is analogous to few-shot warm-starting but operates entirely on the prompt parameters:

from peft import PeftModel, PromptTuningConfig, PromptTuningInit, TaskType
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM

base = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("google/flan-t5-xl")

# Load a prompt trained for sentiment classification
sentiment_model = PeftModel.from_pretrained(base, "./prompt-sentiment")

# Extract the learned prompt embeddings
sentiment_embeddings = sentiment_model.prompt_encoder.default.embedding.weight.data
print(sentiment_embeddings.shape)  # torch.Size([20, 2048])

# Initialise a new prompt for NLI using sentiment's embeddings as starting point
# (useful when tasks share vocabulary and surface forms)
config = PromptTuningConfig(
    task_type=TaskType.SEQ_2_SEQ_LM,
    num_virtual_tokens=20,
    prompt_tuning_init=PromptTuningInit.RANDOM,  # will be overridden manually
)

nli_model = base
nli_model = get_peft_model(nli_model, config)

# Transfer prompt weights from sentiment task
nli_model.prompt_encoder.default.embedding.weight.data.copy_(sentiment_embeddings)
# Then fine-tune on NLI data — converges faster than random init

Transfer between semantically similar tasks (e.g. sentiment → NLI, or summarisation → QA) consistently reduces convergence time by 20–40% compared to random initialisation, even when the downstream task is quite different.

One productive application of prompt tuning is personalised instruction following: each user or user group has their own soft prompt that adjusts the base model's tone, style, or domain focus without modifying shared weights.

# Each user stores a ~80KB prompt file capturing their preferences
# The base 7B model (~14 GB) is shared across all users

def serve_personalised_response(user_id: str, query: str) -> str:
    # Load user's soft prompt (fast — it's tiny)
    prompt_path = f"/prompts/{user_id}"
    personalised_model = PeftModel.from_pretrained(base_model, prompt_path)
    personalised_model.eval()

    inputs = tokenizer(query, return_tensors="pt")
    with torch.no_grad():
        out = personalised_model.generate(**inputs, max_new_tokens=200)
    return tokenizer.decode(out[0], skip_special_tokens=True)

# 10,000 users → 10,000 × 80KB = 800 MB of prompt storage
# vs 10,000 × 14 GB = 140 TB for full model copies

The training dynamics of prompt tuning differ from full fine-tuning in a characteristic way. Because only the prompt embeddings update, the gradient signal must propagate from the loss backwards through all frozen layers to reach the trainable parameters. This long gradient path means:
: with no internal parameter updates to accumulate, the prompt tokens need steeper steps. Learning rates of 0.01–0.1 are typical vs 1e-5 for full fine-tuning.
: a full fine-tuning run might saturate in 3 epochs; prompt tuning routinely needs 10–30.
: because the base weights never change, the model's general capabilities are perfectly preserved. The soft prompt provides additive task steering, not overwriting.
: only the soft prompt parameters accumulate optimiser states. For Adam, this is 2 × l × d × 4 bytes — often less than 1 MB even for large d.
trainable soft tokens at the input embedding layer only — the entire transformer remains frozen.
— 50–100× smaller than prefix tuning, often measured in kilobytes.
Performance matches full fine-tuning only at large scale (10B+). Below that, prefix tuning or adapters are more accurate.
Text initialisation is recommended at moderate model sizes; random initialisation works at large scale.
Prompt ensembling (5 prompts) can outperform a single fine-tuned model with dramatically less storage.
P-Tuning v2 extends prompt tuning to every layer, closing the accuracy gap at smaller model scales at the cost of more parameters (approaching prefix tuning territory).
